facts.text: Espresso is produced by forcing hot water under high pressure through finely ground coffee beans.
Espresso originated in Italy.
A standard espresso serving is typically 25–30 ml.
The foam on top of espresso is called 'crema'.
Espresso is extracted in approximately 25–30 seconds.
Espresso has a viscosity similar to that of warm honey.
A typical 30 ml serving of espresso contains approximately 65 mg of caffeine.
A typical 240 ml serving of drip coffee contains approximately 95 mg of caffeine.
Despite higher caffeine per unit volume, a single espresso serving has less caffeine than a cup of drip coffee.
Espresso contains three distinct phases: an emulsion of oil droplets, suspended solids, and a layer of gas bubbles.
The oil droplets in espresso are perceived as creamy in the mouth.
Modern espresso using hot water under pressure was pioneered by Gaggia in the 1940s.
Gaggia originally called espresso 'crema caffè', meaning 'cream coffee'.
The first Bezzera and Pavoni espresso machines in 1906 took 45 seconds to make a single cup.
The act of making espresso is called 'pulling' a shot, originating from lever espresso machines.
On lever espresso machines, a barista pulls a handle attached to a spring-loaded piston.
The spelling 'expresso' has been used by English speakers in America and England since at least the 1940s.
The word 'espresso' conveys three meanings: pressing out flavor, speed, and doing something expressly for a person.
Espresso per 100 ml contains around 212 mg of caffeine.
Espresso contains significant levels of magnesium, niacin, and riboflavin.
Caffè corretto is espresso 'corrected' with brandy, grappa, or sambuca.
Caffè crema is a 'long' espresso made with more water and coarser grounds, yielding approximately 120–180 ml.
A Freddo espresso is mixed with sugar and ice in a drink mixer.
Viennese coffee consists of 2 shots of espresso topped with whipped cream.
Espresso serves as the base for cappuccino, caffè latte, and americano.
There is no universal standard defining the espresso extraction process.
The Italian Espresso National Institute publishes technical parameters for 'certified Italian espresso'.
It is a widespread myth that espresso must be made with dark-roast coffee beans.
Tamping the coffee grounds promotes even water penetration during espresso extraction.
Dictionary.com attributes the 'expresso' spelling to 'ex-' being a more common word-starting pattern in English.
